summary: A 44-year-old woman recently started on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily for primary hypothyroidism (TSH 11.2, free T4 low-normal) is asking the endocrinology nurse line about how the medication is monitored, when she will feel better, and a few practical questions about how to take it relative to other medications and food. The held-out answer must reflect ATA 2024 guideline framing — TSH-guided dosing, 4-6 week recheck after dose change, target TSH typically 0.4-4.0 mIU/L (or 0.5-2.5 in some patient subsets), morning dosing on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before food and other medications, interactions with calcium / iron / PPIs / coffee, the realistic onset of symptomatic improvement, and the role of levothyroxine alone as standard (rather than combined T3 / desiccated thyroid for routine use).

messages.content: I just started levothyroxine 50 mcg for an underactive thyroid. My TSH was 11. How do I take it correctly, when will I feel better, and how often will my TSH be checked?

do_NOT: describe T3 (liothyronine) or desiccated thyroid as routine first-line
skip the empty-stomach / before-other-medications administration guidance
skip the typical 4-6 week TSH recheck interval after dose change
minimize realistic 6-12 week timeline for symptomatic recovery
describe brand-vs-generic switching as inconsequential without nuance

expected_sources_referenced: ATA Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism — Jonklaas et al., Thyroid 2014 (with 2024 update)
Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline on Hypothyroidism
Centanni et al., NEJM 2006 — gastric pH and levothyroxine absorption
